Never take unfair advantage, meaning it's okay to have them work for free, but like don't have them work for free five nights in a row. It's okay to have them up on a panel, but don't have them do a breakout session for you for free where they're missing the main session. Don't take unfair advantage. Don't booze it up in their presence. I'm the last guy who should be talking about this because for many years I drank, I drank heavily and I drank with and around clients. Not a smart thing to do. If you're drinking, you gotta drink, go drink someplace else. Same advice would have been good for Jimmy Swagger about in your hometown, which brings us to this. Don't date or sleep with clients or clients, family members or employees. Turn it down no matter how generously, freely, liberally it is offered. It's another reason not to drink in their presence because you're more likely not to turn it down. It can never, ever, ever, ever, ever work out well. If you gotta marry it, then stop the client relationship, then date her or date him and be very aware of potential liabilities, this and other kinds. This business is fraught with risk, fraught with risk. Most people don't give it any thought. You're dispensing advice. People are interpreting it, they hear kind of what they want to hear, they hear pieces of it, etc. And they are following it. And then if they get adverse results, regardless of how much of it was actually due to their own pilot error, there is an enormous temptation to attempt holding you responsible. This business is fraught with liability. How do you protect yourself? One selectivity. Who do you deal with? Do you deal with people? Like in my case, as soon as they want their lawyer to look at the contract, I don't want it. That's enough for me. What does it tell me? There's a person of influence in their life who only increases his power and authority and control by getting them to sue people. I'll opt out, thank you very much. That's what I threw at with our big corporate client. Five changes to the contract, you know, the lawyers, assistants, and civilians just not do it. Well, we got to do something to appease them. So we wind up, we change one sentence, which I rewrite everything I get the way they want. And I say, no, we got to have an understanding. None of this in the future. I don't want to be hearing about your attorney. So we agreed, yeah, I'm sorry. But look, the seminar business is, and hardly anybody talks about it, of course, by the way, but the seminar business, for example, is rife with alienation of affection lawsuits. Everybody gets them. There's a couple who get them a lot. But a person comes to the three-day workshop, Mary comes to the workshop. Mary gets all charged up about take charge of your life, get rid of the toxic people in your life. This is the advice we give her. It turns out the most toxic person in her life is her husband. Everybody goes home and gets rid of them. Husband Sue's seminar presenter for alienation of affection. Hey, he's got a case. It happens all the time. So selectivity, control, eliminating problems as soon as you see them, contracts, good disclaimer language. Do not use your clients as your coach, consultant, or therapist. It's a tremendous temptation to tell them your problems right after they've told you their problems. You're not supposed to be confiding in them. Whatever you confide in them should be purposefully and strategically confided in them to increase your control over them, not to unburden yourself. If you need therapy, go hire one, preferably somewhere invisible to your constituency. Don't be using a client's shoulder to tell your stories to. They've got to want to be like you. You may think to yourself, why would they want to be like me, especially if they make more money than I do, especially if they want to be like you because of the behavior they see. The confidence they see, the discipline they see, the control they see, the attitudinal stuff they see. They don't want to be like you if you're weak and crying and complaining and moaning and your relationship screwed up too, your employees are all idiots and your customers are all morons too. So they don't want to be like you then. So you can't be sharing that with them. Do not complain in ways that undermine their desire to be like you. And if you cross the line, do it sparingly and know you've changed the relationship. Here's another big mistake. For those of you with weak blanners, we're 15 minutes away. Big mistake. Something that continued interest and patronage can be earned through either the core value of your competencies and services and so forth delivered or retained as gratitude for contributions you've made to them in the past or based on the loyalty of friendship. So let's work down the list. The first one we've talked about a lot. You're going to keep them for about a year based on the core value of whatever it is that you deliver. If that's all you did. No frills, no fluff, no entertainment, no psychological manipulation, no control, no none of that and you just deliver core values and services, at best you're going to keep them with you for a year. And it won't matter how well they've done or are doing. Too much of the other glue will be absent. They'll leave you no matter what. The second one's gratitude for contributions past. We all think that that's going to happen. Man, over the last five years, think of all the money I made for this person. Or man, when he called up and had that emergency, I jumped on the plane and I rushed in there and I cleaned up that mess. Their memories are remarkably short and they tend to rewrite history in order to reduce the value and amount of the contribution you made. The story I like to tell about it is years ago when I had General Cassette, manufacturing company, we had a speaker client who we produced all this stuff. His name was Keith DeGree. And at the time, Keith has become very successful. But at the time Keith was struggling and sometimes couldn't pay his bills and would get a gig at the last minute and take it and call up and need 3,000 Cassette albums in Tupelo tomorrow and I haven't paid you for the thousand that I already got. But if you'll just give me another cheeseburger, I'll pay you for the cheeseburger I got on Tuesday, whatever the wimpy, the guy in the Popeye cartoon's was. So we put on a night shift even that we couldn't afford to pay him and put on a night shift and get them things done and get him in Tupelo because without him, Keith's going to work and not have any product to sell and not make any money. So we carried him and we bent over backwards for him and we helped him and we created opportunities for him for about a year and a half. One day Keith calls up and wants to take us to lunch. So Pete Lillo and I are in the car and he's taking us to lunch. We think he's taking us to lunch to be a nice guy. How nice? He's in town, he's taking us to lunch. He's taking us to lunch to break the news to us, he's moving all the business to another vendor. Why? Because he's going to save a penny, a cassette. A six tape album, that's six cents. And he said, of course you understand because biz is biz. Never forgot it. Not mad about it, he's right. Biz is biz, that's what the relationship is. Now the decision is still stupid but that's neither here nor there. It is reality. So the idea that you are going to keep them based on contributions passed, we have a saying at the racetrack, what have you done for me lately? Because the nature of race horses is they slow down as they age. Just like we do. So horse made you 30,000 bucks last year, this year he's struggling to finish into money at all. It doesn't matter he made us 30 grand last year. It doesn't matter he's made 300 grand lifetime. What did you do this week? He's eating this week. He ain't eating in our memories. He's eating this week. What have you done for me lately? That's how everyone is. You'll keep them based on the loyalty of friendship. Know it'll work exactly the opposite way as we just discussed. If it turns into friendship, the only way you'll keep them is if you're going to give them everything for free. It's the only way to keep them. And that's not a game that you want to be in. Let's skip a page for a second. We'll stay on retention for a second. Go to page 47. We're going to come back to one. Three most important truths about retention. Clients continue based more on their feelings than on realities including accomplishments and results. Not for me for a guy that's not a touchy feely guy, but break the news to you. We're in a feelings business. It's all about how they feel about us, how they feel about themselves, how they feel about the experience that they're having. Not really about the results. Clients will divorce themselves based on their feelings even in spite of accomplishment and results. Make the guy a million dollars, but he ain't having fun at the meetings. He's gone. Make the guy a million dollars, but he didn't get his moment in the spotlight this month and feels shorted and slighted and didn't get recognition. He's gone. It is the careful assessment control and manipulation of their feelings that is essential to retention and ascension. It's all about how they feel. Not what's actually happening, what has happened, not what the results are. There's got to be results. But the difference between adequate results and superior results do not translate into a difference in retention. That's not where you get it. You get it from how they feel. That's where you get it. So additional mistakes. Delivering too much content. So we're going to try and give them more value. That's how we'll retain them. They're dropping out, let's add four more CDs a month. Let's do six calls instead of four calls. Let's do eight-hour meetings instead of four-hour meetings. Let's do three extra meetings. Let's let them call me in the middle of the night on the cell phone. Let's let them email me. Let's add, let's add, let's add, let's add, let's add. It won't matter. Enough is enough. You may have too little, but at some point, piling on more value won't affect retention. Won't do it. And in fact, too much content. Number one reason why people drop out of any Glaser Kennedy program. Guess what? Too much stuff. I mean, translation, too much value. Give us less value. Then we'll stay. Well, it sounds stupid when you say it that way, doesn't it? Well, it is stupid. But nevertheless, it's reality because see when you give them too much stuff for them and they can't like process it and they can't handle it and it's too much information, then they start to feel inadequate. When people have negative feelings, what do they do? They try and get away from whatever is causing the negative feelings. They don't try to correct their own behavior, by the way. Forget that. They try and get away from whatever is causing the negative feelings. So if you're causing negative feelings, they're going to get away from you. If you're causing positive feelings, then they're going to want to be around you all they can. So you can't give them too much content. Mistake, delivering overly sophisticated or complex content. You can't ask them to think a lot. They don't like it. Diagram with five things on it, okay. Diagram with 15 things on it, too much. I still want it. It is too much. Well if you're too complex, if you're too sophisticated, especially too soon, or with the wrong people who haven't moved up through an essential ladder, you'll drive them away. You'll lose them for all the same reasons. They're embarrassed. They don't understand what they're hearing or reading, but they won't ask. And so now they're not getting value. They feel like a slow student in the room, et cetera. Teaching too much, period. The worst thing in the world you can do in a coaching environment is fall in love with the sound of your own voice. Not why they're there. They may have joined because of you, but that's not what's going to keep them there. They can only take so much. Even in a seminar environment. And I tend to crank it up, say, you guys pay a lot of money to be here. So I think, well, you know, let's work them to death. But you're going to be happy if I work it again. Ten years ago I would have gone until midnight tonight. Now I know. I could go until midnight. You'll be less happy. Even if you've got another whole big notebook and we went to midnight. Mostly you'll be less happy, not more happy. Even though you can sleep when you go home for free, you'll be less happy. They only take so much. And then it's too much. And then everybody's unhappy. So teaching too much, period. Turns into boredom, drudgery. People got to have fun. Especially in a coaching environment. They got to have fun. One way versus cafeteria. You lose a lot of people in coaching. We used to lose them in practice management. If you have one way for them to do something. Here's my successful way. You're going to do it my way. They're happier if you give them a cafeteria. And so they can pick and choose stuff. Now that's not the most effective thing usually you can do for them. I'm going to do you guys after the break. Is that okay? So yeah. So be up here after the break. So usually the most effective thing we can do is give them an absolute model. Conform to this model, boom, you get these results. But that's not what they want. They're not going to be happy about that. And to be fair, different people, different things. It's like that office tour thing. Different offices, different things. It's got to now be tweaked a little bit. Office by office by office by office. Some offices it ought to be the front desk person doing the tour. Because she's good and bubbly and personable and doesn't mind learning the script and will do a good job with it. And other offices, the worst person in the world you can have do the office is Barbara at the front desk. Barbara does a pretty good job at the front desk, but you know, Barbara don't like giving to her. And she's not going to like giving to her. And so you can't dogmatically say tour should be given by main front desk person. That's how we do it. That's how our body does it. Imposing your values on others. We kind of talked about that some. It's about their perceived value, not yours. There. This is actually a very useful list. It's kind of a dumb list, but it's a very useful list. It's worth flying across the country for. There's the things that make them happy. Getting lots of stuff. They like stuff. They may never look at it. They may never use it. They like stuff. My coaching group members, when we had the VIP meetings in the house, you know what they like best? Getting in my way. They like my trash. Oh, God, they love the trash. I never took the, once I figured it out, I didn't take the trash out before the meetings. Normally you clean up. I made a bigger mess. God, they love getting in my trash. What can we find in Dan's trash? Oh, can I get a copy of what you just found in the trash? Can I get a copy of what you just found in the trash? They love what was in my trash. I filled the trash up for them. They love stuff. Our platinum meetings, everybody brings stuff. Everybody hands out stuff. Everybody goes home with stuff. They love stuff. Give them a lot of stuff. They love it. They like taking home stuff. They feel like they went out. They killed something. They brought it back. They only go home empty handed. They come home from a meeting. Somebody says, where were you? I was at the meeting. What did you get? Where did you go? I was at a meeting. What did you get? I got all this stuff. Look at all this stuff. Great. They're happy. They like getting stuff in packages. Huge mistake. I just dealt with a client again, just about did themselves in, switching to electronic delivery of all of their content. Big mistake. Save a lot of money. Save a lot of money by sending that newsletter out electronically or letting you go download it in a PDF file. No good. Package. Christmas morning. Rip it open. What's there? Spread it out. Poke at it. Look at it. Much better. They like getting stuff in packages. They like surprises. Extra book. They didn't expect an article with a handwritten note on top of it. They didn't expect a Kacacca, a postcard when you go on a trip. They like getting surprises. They like new and interesting things to do. Not necessarily the most productive things to do. Even if they haven't implemented the 47 things you already gave them, they need a 48th. You can't keep drilling on the 47. They won't be happy. That might be the most effective thing you could do. It might be the most productive thing you could do, but nothing's productive if you lose them. You can't help them if they're not there. So if you've got 47 good things for them to do and they've only gotten to number 12 and you still got to give them a 48th. You still got to give them a 49th. You still got to give them a 50th. They got to have something new. They want what's new. They don't want what they already got. They don't want what you've already showed them. They don't want what's old, even if the best thing everybody could do is spend time drilling it. They want what's new. If you don't give it to them, they'll go hop to another dog who's waving the next new flavor. I got something new over here. I got something new. We figured I had a multi-level company for a while that I inherited because it's a long story. I had been in Amway, so I already learned the message from Amway. You've got to have a new product every month. Otherwise you don't keep anybody interested. How many products do you need? When I left Amway, we had 4,000 some-hottie products. Our own multi-level company, we had more products than we had distributors. But we had to have a new product all the time. New product, new product, new product. How about let's sell a product we got now? No, a new product. Publishing industry, our publisher. They got to have 50 new books every quarter. How about sell the ones you got? They're only a year old. We've only sold 40,000 copies of them. There's 12 million people. I mean, couldn't we sell some more of those? New title, new title, new title. Now those are smart people running big companies. The behavior doesn't matter. They've got to have something new. Toys, they love toys. Got to give them toys. Things, you know? Quizzes, questionnaires, little pieces of software, squeezy balls. Toys, they like toys. Like them a lot. Being sent on goose chases and treasure hunts. Because activity now masquerades its accomplishment. So here's five websites. Go home and look at these websites. Oh, great. Go home, look at the websites. The guy did something. Activity masquerading is a company. A lot of them have gone on goose chases. See, don't download everything and give it to them. Make them go get it. Make them call over here to get stuff sent to them. They like that a lot. Here's three places to call up and get them to send you their catalogs and their stuff. Because now they get three packages. Oh boy, I got three more packages. Right? Activity that feels like accomplishment. They want accomplishment, but most don't want to do everything necessary to have a lot of accomplishment. A little bit of accomplishment is usually enough to satisfy most people. But they want to feel like they've had a lot of accomplishment. So you've got to design activity that feels like accomplishment. So like getting a USP wrote. The whole session on getting a unique selling proposition. See, that feels like accomplishment. Well, it isn't unless you do something with the unique selling proposition. But it feels like accomplishment because you wrote one and you got one. Good food, especially desserts. The cookies are real important. Very important. At coaching meetings, cookies, brownies, desserts. All the other food gets sucked by the way. As long as the desserts are okay. They'll tell it as long as the desserts are good, they'll be happy. But if you don't have any desserts for them, they'll be unhappy. And then the whole thing is all about positive feelings. Do they feel better about themselves? And do they feel good about the experience? And do they feel good about having been there, been involved for whatever period of time it was? If they do, you got them. This is the stuff that makes people happy. Now, it ain't a real sophisticated list. It's a list, none the less. Next list is almost as useful. What are positive feelings? Here's some examples. You can go stroking, gold stars. Everybody likes gold stars. Here's understand what everybody's still doing. At least males are emotionally arrested, development, what, 12, 13, somewhere around there. Doesn't matter now. They're CEO of a multi-million dollar company. The inner child is alive and well. So this is about, they colored it, they bring it to you, they want you to stick it up on a refrigerator with a magnet and ooh and ah over it. Big disappointment if you don't stick it up on a refrigerator and ooh and ah over it. A lot of value if you do stick it up on a refrigerator and ooh and ah over it. This is more important to different people. But, so I give you a great example. Dr. Nielsen's been around with me for either 22 or 27 years, I forget which. 27. He knows everything I know. And he doesn't need anything more to do anyway. He already has every chiropractic patient in whatever Dink County is in. He can't get any more. He doesn't want any more. He needs, he needs to read next month's No BS marketing letter like I need hemorrhoids. There's no reason for him to continue to give us money. There's no sane reason. There's no logical reason. There's none whatsoever. But he loves seeing his stuff in the newsletter. He just loves it. It just, he, I'm sure he just sits and glows in the dark when he sees it. That's how important it is to him. And I always get notes and stuff about how excited he was to see it in the newsletter. Well, he's got good stuff so it's not particularly painful to make a real point of showing him his own stuff three or four times a year to keep him for 27 years. I mean, it's kind of a small price to pay. All right? But you've got to do it with him, all right? So you've got to create opportunities to show off, to teach from what they bring you. Magazine articles. A lot of people are real good about sending me stuff I would never see otherwise. You know, it's from the Plumber's Journal of Australia. Okay? I don't read that. All right? But a lot of people like, well, send me stuff from like USA Today. All right? I read, you know, like I would probably see that. But the way it is, all right? It's not about did I get it from 18 people? It's not about did I see it anyway? It's about they sent it to me. So if I use it in newsletter, I list everybody who sent it to me, not just one. I take time to send notes back a lot of times. Sometimes I'll just scribble it on the thing. Thanks. All right? Now what I try never to do, what's the worst thing in the world you can do to somebody who is telling you a joke that they've taken a bunch of time and trouble to memorize after they read it and maxed them this month? Jump in with the punchline. All right? Now I happen to know 10,000 jokes. So it's hard for me not to do it. But I try not to. They need me to laugh at their joke. That's a gold star. They came with the thing. Not to put it up on the refrigerator, not good. Acceptance. Leader and peer support. A place where they are unconditionally accepted. Very important. Fraternity. People like being an insider with a bunch of outsiders. They like having what they're in be some sort of a Yonix terminology, a good one, secret society, a status symbol, knowing things other people don't know, having a sense that they are superior to their peers and the people around them purely because they have you. And they're in your group. A womb. Cavits O-line. Everybody's walking around with an umbilical cord in one hand looking for a new place to plug it in. You know, they're looking for a new womb to crawl into. So what does that really mean for most people? It means a place that's secure and a place of optimism, of hope. Things are going to be okay. Things are going to be good. Things are going to be better. Even if you have to talk about things being bad, you've got to put the punch line on if yours's how they're going to turn around and be good. And futurism, anticipation. Something new is going to happen. Something exciting is going to happen. Okay? Last thing. Then we break. Go back to page 46. Important page. It's a list you want to make when you go home. This is your key list of facts. Facts about you that you wish to embed in their heads. Which means you've got to make sure you talk about it all the time. You've got to write about it all the time. You stay congruent with it all the time. It is, in a sense, an extension of the reputation that you have constructed. You need your little fact list. What are the facts about Mike that he wants everybody to get and to have permanently embedded in their head? I have facts, in quote marks, by the way, for a reason. There's facts and then there's facts. These are facts because I say so. In some cases, they weren't facts. When I started saying so, they're facts now. They're the facts I want people to know. I want people to know. These in your list ought to be different. I want people to know I'm in high demand. I'm turning down all kind of stuff. There's a waiting list. You're at the end of the list. If you figure out how to jump the line, God bless you. I want everybody to know that. Everybody. Prospects, new people, public. I want everybody to know that. Which means I've got to work it, having them know it. I want them to know I'm difficult to do business with. It's real important to me because by most people's standards, I am difficult to do business with and I want the luxury of being difficult to do business with. I like being difficult to do business with. So I want them to know it. Therefore when they do business with me and they judge me difficult, I can't say they weren't warned. They already knew. They already got themselves over that by the time they're doing business with me. I want them to know that I am extremely intolerant of having my time wasted. Why do I want them to know that? So I won't waste my time. I want them to be really, really, really respectful of my time. I want them to know that I will terminate a client. I want to know that I'm not above fire on somebody for bad behavior and that I can be arbitrary about it. I want them to know that I'm the highest priced guy there is. Why do I want them to know that? To reduce the sticker shock when I quote the fee. Still there by the way, but it's not as bad. And if it's really an issue, see if they're not prepared to spend a lot of money, might as well get that out early so they can go someplace else. Because why do I want to talk to them if they're not prepared to spend a lot of money? So I want them to know I'm the highest priced guy. And of course I want them to know that people are making enormous sums of money as a result of hanging around me. Now those are my facts. You may argue if you wish about to what degree they are facts, but that's my facts list. You too need a facts list. And now that facts list, you are consciously deliberately strategically reinforcing it every opportunity. Everything you write, every meeting, every phone conversation, every coaching thing, every consulting day, every time you meet with a client, it's almost like at the end of this time together how many of those facts did I reinforce at least once? Check, check, check, check, check. I don't do that consciously anymore. Right? I don't have to. But you could start doing it consciously. Check list, conversation. Okay? How many of these things did I manage to work into the conversation? It's kind of like I took a Latin in high school, which there's not much you could do that it would be more useless than taking a Latin in high school. But I took a Latin in high school because I thought it was going to be a veterinarian and at that time you needed Latin to get into veterinary medicine school at Ohio State. That's why I took it. Right? But the only thing I remember from Latin is quovatis. So I go to a lot of trouble to work quovatis into conversations because I paid a heavy price to get it. I had a hard time passing a Latin. So I like to see how many times a year I could work quovatis in somewhere because it's like all I got from the whole class. So I make a deliberate sort of intent. I've ingrained it. I do it automatically now just like I just did it. So I managed to work into a $10,000 seminar, a discussion of quovatis. That ain't easy. You got to work at that. Same thing here. How many of the points on your facts list can you work into every conversation, everything you write, every issue of every newsletter, every CD, every coaching session, every time they interact with you so that you're covering the bases again and again and again and again and again. Why do people know these things about me? Because I make sure they know these things about me. All right. Let's take a break. 15 minutes and the Q&A guys come back up to the table when the break is over. One thing you've got to control here is this is the, well it used to be Greenspan, has indigestion today and the interest rate goes up by a quarter and everybody wants to call and talk to you. That's got to be headed off in advance. Look, I think a lot of that is about how you position yourself to them, which if you don't want to grow staff and you don't want to provide ready access and service and people for them to talk to and so forth, then it's got to be about, you know, you're a little gnome in the cave who's the genius with the financial formula and you only take a select number of clients and you don't have staff and you're not going to have staff. And you know, the trade off of being able to have you is this. If you want that, then go down to the local storefront stock brokerage and deal with a bunch of buffoons. You know, I mean, it really is about positioning now. It's a boutique practice. It's an elite practice. It is built for a small number of individuals. I'll say something interesting about the phones. In my office, and this goes back now before the relationship with Bill, so this is when we had product sales and all sorts of other things. We used to answer the phones live five days a week, normal hours, right? And my mother was the receptionist and she died and didn't have two of them, only had one. So temporarily, you know, we're like covering, so we went to three days a week and I think short hours on those three days. And then people took turns, right? You know, I was a little slow to start looking for a replacement and running ads and then I'm trying to find out how is it affecting us? Well, other than the three people who are covering being grumpy, it isn't affecting us at all. It's not having any impact at all on the business. So I'm thinking, why do I want a full-time, you know? So then we went to two days, then we went to one day, now we're at no days. Now the product stuff has moved. I wouldn't be doing it if I had product sales still. But so now, for me, we're at no days. You know, she never answers. And it doesn't make any difference? No. But again, it is what they expect, right? And so like I do business and I would be prone to do business with your clients where I do, I do business entirely by phone appointment. So somebody's got to request it, have it pre-arranged. And like in my case, I'm blocking about a day and a half a month when all my phone appointments get scheduled on a clump so I can get it all, you know. So like the day, if you guys are Go Plus members, which I assume everybody is, the day we do the Go Plus call, that's a day-in-phone day. I've had phone appointments since seven o'clock in the morning all day long and they, and they may be clients we're working with, they may be new prospects, they may be whatever, and Vicki clumps them all into one day. And so I have like my one day of misery and then I don't have to talk to anybody for like two weeks, right? And so if it's two weeks away and they got to have a phone appointment, they fit into the next available day on which there's a phone appointment. The way that is, if you will, sold to people is the obvious benefit is no phone tag, no me chasing you, you chasing me, so no frustration about that, no uncertainty, and you got my undivided attention for the amount of time that we got blocked for your phone appointment. You know what the deal is, I know what the deal is, you're organized, I'm organized, you got me, right? So even people for whom this is very strange. They get it and they adapt to it. It's mainly about explaining it to them, you know, to start with. And if you have a client base now where they, where you feel like they're going to feel like something's being taken away from them, then at the same time give them something new that is not painful to you. The monthly client tell a seminar group, tell us it so, give them some new benefit at the same time you change the rules and they feel like there's something being taken away from them. But see, so my option would be like your option. My other option would be to attempt to deliver the other kind of service and deliver it badly because I'm not willing to staff to deliver it well. So rather than doing that, I'd rather create something I can deliver and then train them to accept that. So now they're getting exactly what they were promised. I mean precisely. I'm Southwest. Mike. One of the things we talked about was no free lunch and some different things like that. I'm using the most incredible free gift ever, of course, as an IVA and I use it even in my niche market where I give them three months for free of our newsletter and some different pieces of that. How do we balance that out with staying the guru? Well first of all it's product versus manual labor. That's one thing. Second of all it really isn't free as you very well know. I believe at the moment it's $19.95. It's just free because we say it's free. So there is a little skin in the game but if you will it's your low end point of entry. So let's translate it to something that would be a very bad idea. That would be three free months of attending chapter meetings without ever joining. It would be come to three coaching meetings and then make up your mind whether you're going to join. The worst thing is a lot of free idea meetings. The advertising industry revolves around free pitches. So Burger King, like once a quarter, invites all the ad agencies to come in and pitch them and they have a pitch day or pitch week. All the ad agencies prepare all kinds of advertising for nothing and come in there and do dog and pony shows and half the time Burger King stays with the agency they already got and just gives them the best ideas they see during the pitch week. The other half they moved to one but 20 of them did all this work for nothing. As soon as I saw it it made no sense to me. So I rearranged things not to do them. So there's a big difference between sending somebody a free lunch and going to lunch with them for free. So I mean if you want to really go to use that example I wouldn't necessarily mind sending somebody a DVD player with a DVD in it to watch a presentation on something and having a local deli deliver a box of sandwiches so they can sit down and watch a DVD and enjoy lunch as long as I'm not going and doing a free lunch with them. Okay this is a control question. When you have the maybe 100 plus area exclusive coaching people how are you projecting control when you don't want to physically get them all in a room together but you want to keep control and I know there's takeaways and things. Are you talking about the end user or are you talking about your consultants? You're talking about your consultants. Well talk to Howard. Cats. Rules monitoring group delivery of information training reminding of rules. You will have to get them all together in a room from time to time. It's unavoidable. If they can be mystery shopped they should be mystery shopped to check compliance or non-compliance. You've got to be willing to throw them out and they got to know it. And you will always have somebody wandering off the reservation. It's inevitable. And you go and you get them back and they go do something they're not supposed to be doing you take it away from them and you slap their hand and you bring them back. But if you've got a hundred consultants using your system if you've got a hundred franchise owners if you've got a hundred sales people a hundred distributors whatever terminology you want to go use on anyone given day well first of all the thing that helps a lot is that on anyone given day a big number of them are doing nothing. So they can't get in too much trouble doing nothing. But of the ones who are doing something about a third of them will be trying to reinvent your business instead of implementing the stuff they're supposed to be implementing. And a third of them are going to be so far off the reservation you won't even know what business they're in. And you got to keep catching them and rounding them up. And if their productivity warrants it fine if their productivity doesn't you know then you got to change the program or you got to get rid of them. By the way with them or with clients you have unilateral rules you truthfully has invisibly has possible and force them differently on different people. Jimmy Johnson when he was on a Peter Loew event and other coaches have told their version of the same story. Jimmy said you know he said if Emmett Smith is late for or let's say he falls asleep during a meeting somebody gently goes over wakes Emmett up gets him a cup of coffee and if he really can't stay awake we get him a pillow and we help him lay down on his desk. If the third string lineman who missed six blocks last week falls asleep in the meeting I kick the desk over and trade his ass to the Vikings. Now we do it so we don't advertise it we try and do it invisibly but sure is there a client who has been with me for 15-20 years who pays $100-$200,000 a year to be with me does he get a little more slack than the new guy or the guy who only spent 20 or the guy who is in a coaching group or of course of course. Same thing true with franchisees or sales people. Okay newsletter. You talk about. Lean into the mic so they can hear you. Newspaper newsletter you change up different things you want every year you're creating something new like within your groups how about within your newsletter do you change the entertainment value what are you looking at with the change. The formula never the formula doesn't change there's a formula to it X number of words to this X number of words to that sort of the structure a little checklist of things you want to hit on every month those formulas generally don't change but yeah you try and do a little something not necessarily per calendar year but every so often that so you do a special issue on a given topic or you print an issue on pink paper or you send candy with it or you put a DVD in with it this month or you print it upside down or sideways or you know you do some of those kinds of things. The unique thing about a newsletter as opposed to saying needing to make changes in the other deliverables to feel fresh so the coaching thing has to change almost meeting to meeting to meeting and program to program to program and year to year to year a little. One of the important things about the newsletter is its constancy its consistency so you're balancing that with the need to every so often surprise them a little. Thank you. Hey there. To avoid overly complex and sophisticated material we talked about should you coach the lowest common level and have the high performers who get it be bored or push the envelope and let the non performers struggle and potentially drop out. Depends on what you want to accomplish. Look if you if you go all the way down to the lowest common denominator. Well first of all some of that some of the answer of course depends on how big the spread is of intelligence competence and experience of the people that you have a coaching group. So to use me as an example and I don't have them anymore but so the gold VIP groups versus the platinum group. The platinum group first of all they're all in the same next they're all information marketers so that VIP groups were butcher baker candlestick maker mixed breed of dog. So it's easy in a sense it's easier when they're all in one thing the harder part is if they don't participate or if they're all actually doing exactly the same thing. And they're all five to fifty million dollar a year guys mostly in the middle of that 101520 so there's not huge financial disparity. There's not huge experience disparity and usually each year I got I make sure I have one slower more of a beginner somebody in there to ask the dumb questions and be helpful but I don't slow it down for him. He's he's got to catch up. Now the gold VIP I kind of took a pulse of who's there and then you had to like bring it down. If you bring it you're right if you bring it down too far of course you really frustrate the smart ones. And the real answer to that is to have a place for them to go. So it sets up your ascension. So for example if you look at my transition to move in the VIP groups to bill was I went from four groups to one VIP millionaires group where there was a income requirement to be in the group. And I basically sift and sorted and took the smartest pencils out of the four boxes and moved them into one and sent the other three to go play him. And he's since sifted and sorted and re juggled them you know twice. But you're right it's a constant problem. And sometimes you don't know it. You know you thought they were all and then you discovered they aren't. And sometimes you got to make one leave. You know the way to solve the problem is somebody who's either really behind the curve or somebody who's way above them has to literally be taken aside and said look you know this just really isn't for you or it's not really for you right now. If you got something else appropriate for them great. If you don't it's here's your feedback. You know here's where you need to get to next year or look you really need to wait until we have a really advanced program and we don't you know. Okay. All right. Let's look at some of the sample documents. Some of them are of minor importance and some of them are of significant importance but let's start on page 172. So if you jump all the way back to 172 you're going to find a page that looks like this. This happens to be my 2007 coaching call schedule which is in that gee it seemed like a good idea to fit it all in one day at the time. But yeah what's kind of important about this is to mention to you is that they don't pick that will drive you nuts. You start to run any kind of a like democracy approach to running coaching groups where they're getting to pick slots pick who goes in what order at your meetings any of that kind of stuff. This is all assigned. So when they join a group or when we renew a group or we're getting a group organized for the year they are allowed to express their preferences and then I ultimately assign them their time slots. Now I try and be sensitive to their preferences. I try and be sensitive to time zones but I also am very sensitive to my own priorities. So for example on a day like this I know where my energy laws are going to be. I put people there intentionally who I know are going to pick me up not be difficult or dull or whatever. I know a few people who are going to miss their calls two times during the year three times a year four times a year. They're going to Jason to a break. I don't care what time slot they want it because if they haven't called in in the first five minutes odds are they ain't calling in any other 15 guess what I just doubled my break time. So and I got to put real sharp pencils at the end because I'm like dying at the end of the day so I got to have people there that those calls are a real pleasure and they're really organized and they got a lot going on and you know. So just as a example it affects everything that you do. You got to take into consideration the things that they want but ultimately you build it to suit you. It doesn't matter whether it's a call schedule it's a meeting it's an agenda you know whatever you build it to suit you. Not sure why I had that first but this is this goes back a ways if you flip back we're jumping around if you flip back to page 131 you're going to see a page like looks like this. Now this was this was from the OVIP groups and again we gave this to them and let them give first preference second preference third preference got all their preferences in some consideration to first come first served but again ultimately building it to suit me more than to suit anybody else. I'll tell you for example if you look at page 156 here's the 2006 list for day two there were two days back to back. The second one needed a lot of help and was sort of the weak link of the group I mean no disrespect it's just but was sort of the weak link of the group so I had to put her between two people I was really going to enjoy talking to and we're going to make me work too hard and we're going to come to the table with a lot of good stuff. If I put three weak links together I'm irritable by the time I'm dealing with the third one it comes through in your voice you know you're liable not to deal with them well you know etc etc again there's one on here I guess I won't point them out but there's one on here who pretty much missed his call almost every month and had done so the previous year so he got put next to a break which buys me you know a bigger break. stuff organized my's pretty simple by the way everybody's got a drawer and so that stuff accumulates in their drawer as the month goes by and then we stick it on a big envelope and we send it to them time is fast disappearing etc etc etc now that whole paragraph what that actually provokes is a immediate little rush of additional bookings for consulting and copywriting I don't remember why I actually did it at the moment I think I just gone and bought a 75,000 horse if I'm not mistaken but but but that whole paragraph of there's no time left and you know I really can't work anymore and etc so what that really does is provoke a bunch of people now who hurry up and want you to do stuff um it's sort of primitive but effective reverse psychology this is a little control memo and it's it's interesting literally only because of its first line this is called negative optioning it just makes your life easier um this is unless any of you has to be left out I do that a lot where here's what I'm going to do if you don't want me to do it or you don't want to be a part of it you've got to now come back and tell me otherwise it's happening then I don't have to wait for their responses I don't have to worry about who hasn't responded I don't have to chase answers and I do a lot of stuff I do like if I'm somebody sent in a testimonial and I'm going to use it in the next issue of the newsletter I don't fax them and ask them to fax be back and tell me it was okay I fax them and say unless I hear you screaming sometime in the next 10 days I'm using it so it cuts one whole step out of the communication process you know in order to do it that way it's just another example this is platinum 07 so this is a memo uh that went to platinum members it shows them what their call dates are it shows them their call times um the call preparation paragraph it's 166 I'm sorry it's page 166 should you want to have notes or materials in front of me for your call those materials must be in So that's the same thing with these people Yeah What I wanted to ask was the idea of a renewal every year versus Just getting their credit card and mastermind groups and just keeping on billing them so they don't have to make a decision It's actually a more complicated question than you might think There's much to be said for there never being a renewal Obviously, you don't have to go through a renewal process. You don't have to wait. They got to opt out etc. I Would be more prone to do it with a big coaching program With 75 80 a hundred people in the group that I would with the small ones And I'll tell you why And again, this is me personally Number one is every year. There's somebody I want to leave Okay, and I have others I want in so I want an opportunity to juggle it Secondly every year I raise the fee So the continuity to me is a big trade-off against leaps and fees So that's an issue And There's no law that says you couldn't have one one way and one the other If you have more than one program So one program could be continuity another program could be you know renewable fee But when you do renewable fee you got to do it early not late You know, you can't be renewing at the end There's pros and cons to both Pinch 167 I don't really know You don't even need to look at that let's look at 168 168 is real interesting because you will find meeting rules of conduct So the whole paragraph on 168 now spells out, you know what the rules are And so how many staff people you can bring Now you'll see this is our Emmett Smith conversation. We'll send you another facts That's the way we used to handle clients and prospective clients and I'd sit there with the list Check them off, you know and some of the course would leave messages and then a week later be how come he didn't Because it says right there. He wouldn't gonna call you back. You either got through or you didn't get through Now as goofy as that sounds it was pretty efficient actually and I Mean I Prospective clients fortune 1,000 companies doesn't make an inference. Well the rest of you it's a good book to read It's a fun book to read and if you view it as a sales training Book rather than what it has written as Well, I mean it's for those of you don't know Neil there's a whole industry and subculture of how to meet women teachers and experts and gurus and practitioners and it's a whole World unto itself You might know Been a member of ours for years came to a copywriting seminar mine years ago Ross Jefferies who's like kind of the evil one of the tribe His thing is speed seduction Well, this is an adult group his guarantee actually I'll tell you something very funny about Ross Jefferies I'm doing this real small copywriting seminar And I didn't really screen anybody who was there and the deal was everybody brings their stuff and everybody shows their stuff And we all workshop it, you know together and Ross Jefferies is registered and But there's also this so there's one woman in the crowd I don't I think we had 15 people to seminar there's one woman and she's like a little lady type woman and She's like so I forget what she's doing. I think she's doing like She's selling like quilting kits and stuff And she's in the same she's probably stuff with her well Ross's deal his slogan is if you don't get laid I don't get paid and I Mean, I don't really need to go farther than that, you know, but Ross is really oily and deliberately offensive and profane and And it is it speeds adoption what it is what it says. I mean his deal is no date no meal No, nothing, you know pick up sack and So I get to the meeting room nice and she's people introducing themselves and I'm thinking oh, this is not good This woman is just gonna be Upside down offended, you know, and she's got you know a cross on her neck and she's I mean you just know this is not gonna be pretty You know She had the most fun with him She was totally into working on his copy and he treated her well and she just had a blast, you know I'm sure she never admitted to anybody what she did for two days, but So there's this whole soap culture of these people and Neil Strauss writes for the Rolling Stones some good writer Neil sort of went underground with them for an extended period of time became one of them and then writes this book all about them and How they do what they do and so it's very well done, but I mean it's it is Has Sidney's original book was the Mayfarm Adam it is what I call an unintentional sales training book It wasn't written for that purpose But if you read it for that purpose In addition to it being a good read unlike laws where you got a wade in addition to it being a good read It's a great sales training book. How'd you all wind up on one side of the room? That's okay. Well quick pitch for those of you that don't Jerry is Well Jerry's deceased for one thing, but Jerry's one of the the sources I found real early and Jerry was doing a lot of what I've done and what we've done with the inner circle business Really before anybody now. He never really understood how to turn it into big money But he had all the mechanics right And so if you study Jerry stuff You immediately see you'll see what I stole because it's blatantly obvious But I sort of learned the personality marketing thing. He's one of the ones I studied I learned the inner circle thing. He's one of the ones I studied I learned how to do a newsletter that worked and helped people's interest by studying Jerry And it's really regrettable that he didn't know a few things that would have turned it into big money But it is an enormously good resource. I forget the I wanted to write the years down. So this is What I got for is all of his newsletters for four years from 1992 to 1996 Which arguably probably was his heyday Although he dates back earlier than that And he advertised on the opportunity magazines some of the business magazines and the writers magazines is where his leads came from And he had a thing called towers club for writers His his original pitch sold a nine dollar book the writers utopia formula how to make $500 a day I think writing and never being published by a publishing house Jerry actually started the information business selling a little book about how to get gophers out of your gardens and Would classify that And then he developed from writers He really was teaching information marketing before anybody was calling it information marketing And then had a second inner circle club about that and a second newsletter about that So all of his newsletters what's in that resource are all of his newsletters from 92 to 96 And there's some notes in front by me Calling your attention to different things in all of them and the significance of them and how you might use them There's the original thing he delivered that sold people into his inner circle the writer's utopia report There's his other book about how to start and publish newsletters Which was like my little guide when I started to write and publish new news news Newsletters and their samples of some of his sales letters The original sales letter for his coat for his first coaching group And so forth so in terms of bulk you can say it's about the a thick and It's It's been a real important resource for me It's also sort of you know classic fine that you probably would never see on your own, but a serious information marketer You will love it to have fun with it and benefit from having it and sort of see Where some of these ideas were sourced from and then how they matured and developed from the very primitive beginnings There's a lot of stuff to be learned by studying Jerry and so I suggest it to you as a resource Has a corollary one of the book I didn't mention but there's a book called What a way to make a living by Lyman wood how many own that puppy Not many okay fine that can you get that at Amazon does anybody know is it still alive? It is okay, so go to Amazon and get that one That's a goodie and that is really Well, it's such an interesting story. You got to go get it But but there's great examples in it and it really is about a guy Was very primitive tools none of the tools. I mean these people predate, you know, no facts no email no internet You know no tell a seminars None of the tools we have available today Rounding up a herd creating ascension keeping them involved for years Creating coaching groups creating client groups. They were way ahead of the curve and Lyman would amongst other things created a religion So that's really a very interesting book and those of you that are really into this will Like it and find it both interesting and entertaining and useful as well Ben Suarez his book seven steps to financial freedom, which is a terrible title But is a book that you ought to have as well because it really is about the mail order business and the In some respects the information marketing business and the lead generation business and all of that Those two don't relate as directly to what we're talking about here has does my Buchanan collection But they sort of connect to it and they really relate to the information marketing business So that's that's Reach resources. There is a order form there for the Buchanan stuff if you don't have it and you want to get it Give it to me The other thing that's on there most of you probably know about it But there's an entire box that came out of a one-time meeting I did for doing the area exclusive type coaching programs where they're getting exclusivity and How to structure all of those and how to sell those if you don't own that you may want that as well Okay, let's go into section four We'll work for about another half hour here and then we'll wrap up the day with Question and answer let's go into section four And go to our next big success asset It's a To give credit where credit is due it's a halberd term Like Gary Halbert story how many of you even know how many don't know Gary is Okay, my Gary Halbert story. Well, I could tell you a number of funny stories, but my Gary Halbert story first Gary Halbert seven hour ago, too I Don't know the year but a while ago It's in Key West Florida. It's a seven thousand dollar per person seminar in the room is full. I don't know there may be three four hundred people and The first thing is you probably know getting to Key West is not you know the most convenient thing in the world to do for most people and I said to him, you know, she she Would have killed you to drive up and hold the thing in Miami where it'd be easier for one of us to get to In case it didn't matter There's no point making it easy for him the same number come whether it's easier or hard. So I do it where I wanted it Well, I was a big lesson there So then they're all paid seven thousand dollars and there to speak and and Howard walks up And He did at least 15, maybe 20 minutes at the start of the seminar on the 522 reasons why he didn't want to climb. And that's not why we're here. And don't bother me any time during the seminar. And don't try and hire me to do anything. And don't try and try to, don't bother me at the restaurant. And good 15 to 20 minutes on why he didn't want to do anything or why he didn't want to be hired, how he couldn't hire me, if he tried, and not to bother me, not to try and hire me. And then he went from that into all of the evils of clients, which is one of the reasons he didn't want to work, is how the bad people hate the clients. And he went through his 10 ways copyrights don't be paid, which is client number one, client doesn't implement, number two, client doesn't implement, number three, client doesn't implement, number four, client doesn't implement. Number five, client implements and screws everything up. Number six, client implements and screws. And so he went through his whole rift on how evil and stupid and miserable clients are. And clients suck. And if I never have a client again for the rest of my life, as long as I live, it's okay with me. And I'm sitting there the whole time and I'm thinking, Nero Wolf, the son of him, he's just doing Nero Wolf. And it was brilliant. Well, now for the remainder of the three days, what is everybody trying to do? I mean everybody. People who didn't have a project, they're trying to hire them. They're chasing them into the men's room. They're chasing them around at night. They're waiting by the elevator in the morning for him to emerge from the elevator and elbowing each other aside to try and convince him to let them hire him. This is the craziest thing I've ever seen. And we had lunch every day up in his suite and I said, for real? He said, nah, nah, nah, nah. He said, I need some clients. He said, I'm hoping to come out of here with 10 or 12. He said, you just saw me this morning. That's the best way to get him. He said, trust me, a lot easier to get him this way than trying to get him. He said, I'll get him. And I mean, they badgered him and said, nah, nah, I don't like your idea. That is a dumbest idea. I don't like your idea. The other deal, he's got 22, 23 clients. They're just throwing money at him. And it was a really, really, really good object lesson that I have not forgotten. But anyway, in one of our conversations, Halbert's term is, you know, he said it's real important for you to understand you're not in the copywriting business. Now, you can substitute consulting, you can substitute coaching, if you like. He said, it's very important for you to understand you're not in the copywriting business. He said, because copywriters don't make any money. He said, you're in the self-aggrandizement business. He said, if what you happen to deliver is copy, that's fine. He said, but you're in the self-aggrandizement business. And he said, you want him buying you, not what you do. And then the second thing he said to me is, he says, in order to do it well, you need no conscience. And I would modify it to, you know, the conscience of P.T. Barnum with the egress and the live mermaid and dead hippo, slept around for six weeks while they rounded up a new live hippo. In case you don't know it, dead hippos can float, which is kind of interesting. So if you put them in salt water, they float. So the hippo still looks like he's alive. I understand they're doing the same thing with James Brown. Tell me you haven't thunk it. Tell me you haven't thought about it. So if you really understand everything we're doing, you're in the self-aggrandizement business. I mean, you're in the business of selling you. The deliverables are totally secondary to them deciding they want you. And in the consulting business, if you sort of take a look at the traditional consulting business, it's all about proposals and action plans and what we're going to do for you and all that stuff, and they make small money. The $50,000 a year average in the coaching business is because they're selling the coaching. That's why it's because they're selling the coaching. And you don't want to be selling the coaching and you don't want to be selling the consulting. And in my case, you don't want to be selling the copywriting. You want to be selling the self-aggrandized you, not the deliverables. And it was a really, really good lesson. An example of doing it, I call it the fine art of making mountains out of molehills, I had for years, well, I did a brochure. I hadn't done a brochure in a long time. And I did a brochure in 1996. I didn't really need one, but to this day, I can't tell you why I did it, but I did a brochure. And then, because I didn't need it, even though I called it an annual report, every year then I just updated it with a sticker and stuck a page in the back. I kept using the same one until I ran out of them and then I haven't had one since. But one of the cool things that I did, and I'll tell you where I got it from, I got it from a toilet paper commercial. And I don't remember now the exact commercial, but somehow they talked about how, you know, ten of their rolls of toilet paper would wrap around a globe five hundred times and get you to Mars or some damn thing. They had some kind of statistical stuff. And so I had these facts, you know, number of speeches, number of different cities, largest audience, number of consulting days in a year, number of infomercial industry days in a year. Now, when you think about it afterwards, what difference does it make, how many cities I've been in? What relevance does that possibly have to you and the value you're going to get by hiring me? What difference does it make if I've been in 42 cities, 420 cities, or two cities? Doesn't make any difference at all. But it's an interesting statistic. It builds the panel of statistics up. It makes it sound sort of like, geez, he's in demand and he's running all over the place. Everybody wants it. I have my big list of speaking schedule, you know, clients, people I've been with. But it was all largely mountain out of molehill stuff. I mean, because truthfully in 96, other than the Peter Lowe van, I wasn't working all that much. Well, I was working, but I wasn't working all that much. But I made it look like I was working nonstop every minute of the day. I've made much over the years, continue to make much of, having one of my books be on ink magazines list of 100 best business books. I don't mention they redo the list every year. It sounds like it was like the list of the 100 best business books. They do it every year. So I was on it like once. And I think I was number 92 or 93. I don't mention that fact either. I don't mention the fact that the list is compiled by a freelance writer who I had an ongoing relationship. Kind of, he wrote about me in a bunch of different magazines. And we had sort of a separate financial arrangement. I don't mention that. I mean, there's, you know, well, I mean, you don't need to... One of my earliest mentors tells this story about how he would... It is Junior High... It is school in rural Alabama. The dentist or the dental hygienist would come to class and talk to everybody about dental hygiene. And she picked him out and complimented him on how great his teeth looked. And you know, what a great smile he had and everything. And, you know, obviously you're brushing twice a day and you're flossing and you're doing all the stuff you're doing. And he piped up and said, I brush my teeth once a week, you know, whether they need them or not. And she said, you know, you don't need to tell all you know. And so omission is valuable in and of itself. You don't need to tell all you know. But I've beat that fact up now. And it's a fact. It's just, you know, there's context missing. But I've beat that up for a long time. I mean, everybody, and if you look at all my promotional materials, there's a long list of stuff that are really all molehills made into mountains. The one that just happened, which is a good example of how I would use it if I wanted to bother to use it, is the... I'm in this year's Celebrity Black Book, which is this big thing they sell. It's a member of ours, Jordan McCauley, which now gives this away a lot. So this is like a book, a resource book you use if you're trying to find a celebrity's address and their agent and their contact information. You might want to hire them, whatever. And it's this big, thick thing and everybody and their brothers in there. And I'm in this year's Celebrity Black Book. Well, why am I in there? I'm in there because Jordan McCauley's a member of ours and because I plug his book four or five times. So everything's in there and two-pointing visible anyway, like the white pages. So him sticking in there was no... me in there was no skin off his nose, but it was pretty smart on his part, you know, to do it. So he created currency. So I'll plug his book some more, right? Now, if I really wanted to use that, I would beat the crap out of it. I would magnify it to, you know, named to the 15th or 16th or how many years he's been in business, annual edition of the Celebrity Black Book, along with John Travolta, this person, that person, you know, and I'd list 15, 20 celebrities and dignitaries. And I'd make it sound like it was, you know, who's who of America in the way that I used it. I mean, it's a molehill turned into a mountain. Use everything you got. Use every part of the pig. Don't overlook anything. And as you get better stuff, you get to drop off some of the, you know, some of the lesser stuff. But like you got, what did you get? Direct marketer of Tucson or something? What? Arizona direct marketer of the year. Well, see, that's a... you know, if you aren't using that, that's a big deal. It was just took turns. Yeah, well, three of them took turns. Yeah, I get it. But no, the truth is you got it because they felt guilty after 12 years or something and you spent a lot of money in the major director. You know, but still, it's applauded. It's a credit, you know, and you don't have to say what year and you don't have to say that the direct marketing chapter only had eight people in it. And you don't have to say any of that. You know, you just embellish what you can embellish. So self-aggrandizement, one of the techniques is making mountains out of molehills. Here's some other be somebody strategies. Peter brilliantly used what I call the with strategy or the surrounded by strategy. And again, I mean no disrespect and I don't think he would contradict me, but Peter has never been. And although he has improved, he is never going to be a really great speaker. And the likelihood of Peter getting on a giant stage in front of giant audiences, unless he owns the circus, not good. And he knew it. So he knew if that's what he wanted to do, which is what he wanted to do, he needed to own the circus. But then, even if you own the circus and you pack the tent with 35,000 people and then a little short redheaded, howdy-duty looking guy walks out there and delivers, you know, a so-so speech with so-so charisma. That's not going to last very long. And really his credibility was, I mean, he didn't have much of a story to tell. He'd been a reasonably successful salesperson who decided to be a sales trainer and started doing sales training seminars by running ads in the newspaper and doing three hour, $35 seminars and selling some stuff. And that's it. I just gave you the whole story. It's not like he was Hewlett Packard's number one salesperson or, you know, made a million dollars in a year in selling or any of that. No such story. So you got no story. You got no credibility. And you're not necessarily real good. What's the strategy here? Well, if you surround yourself with big people, you rent the aura, you borrow the aura rather than create the aura. And so Peter brilliantly surrounded himself with all manner of celebrities and top speakers. All it required was writing a check, which a lot of everything in life all it requires is writing a check to get in or get out or to create or whatever. And so his ascendancy and the achievement of his particular goals began by hooking himself to zig and then by hooking himself to celebrities. And the big breakthrough in his case was Ronald Reagan. A whole lot of people didn't want to do those rallies until Reagan did those rallies. And once Reagan did those rallies, then it was like, okay, for everybody to do those rallies. And in fact, then a whole bunch of people wanted to do those rallies and were banging on the door to get them. So Peter used the with strategy, who am I with? Who I am with defines who I am. And that's a very powerful and useful strategy that can be applied many different ways. And so if you think about who I'm with is who I am. So who you interview, who you co-author a book with, who you hire to appear with you on an event or at your house party, et cetera, et cetera, who you're with defines who you are. And Peter used the strategy, Bill. Thank you.
